sanskrit:
मृत्युः सर्वहरश्चाहमुद्भवश्च भविष्यताम्।
कीर्तिः श्रीर्वाक्च नारीणां स्मृतिर्मेधा धृतिः क्षमा॥
transliteration:
mṛtyuḥ sarva-haraś cāham udbhavaś ca bhaviṣyatām
kīrtiḥ śrīr vāk ca nārīṇāṃ smṛtir medhā dhṛtiḥ kṣamā
literal_meaning: Death the all-seizing, and I am; the birth of things to come; fame, beauty, speech among women; memory, intelligence, steadfastness, patience.
contextual_meaning: Krishna names the forces that swallow and seed life, then lists seven feminine archetypes—qualities that allow psyche to hold, remember, and endure. Death is not an enemy but the Self’s own rhythm of closure; the generative spark is equally the Self. The ‘feminine’ list maps ego-resources that keep identity coherent across time: public image (kīrti), bodily presence (śrī), articulate voice (vāk), recall (smṛti), discernment (medhā), affect-regulation (dhṛti), and frustration-tolerance (kṣamā). Owning them as aspects of one Consciousness dissolves the split between life-giver and life-taker, between masculine agency and feminine containment.
psychological_state: Post-dissociative integration: the warrior relaxes defensive hyper-masculinity, sees that what he splits off (death, patience, receptivity) is inside his own wholeness; ego expands to hold both urgency and spaciousness.
trigger_scenario: A high-achieving woman miscarries and feels her identity shattered; therapy reveals that her self-worth had been pinned only on productivity. Recognizing patience, memory, and speech as divine qualities within—not external trophies—she begins to grieve without self-erasure.
anchor_text: I am the close and the spark, the poise that lets you remember and endure.
